content.text: SOC 2 Control Mapping for Claude Operations

SOC 2 is a voluntary attestation framework published by the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA). When an organization uses Claude in a service that touches customer data, regulators and enterprise buyers increasingly require a SOC 2 Type II report covering the AI controls — not just the surrounding infrastructure. Mapping Claude-specific controls to Trust Services Criteria (TSC) is the first step to building an audit-ready control set.

This sub-subtopic walks through each of the five TSC categories, identifies which Claude operational controls satisfy each criterion, specifies the evidence artifacts auditors expect, and provides the compliance code patterns that generate that evidence automatically.

attrs.alt: SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria to Claude Control Mapping

content.text: SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria Overview

The AICPA's Trust Services Criteria consist of five categories. Security (via the Common Criteria) is mandatory for every SOC 2 examination; the other four are elected based on how the service is used:
Category
Code
Mandatory?
Applies When Claude Is Used For…
Security
CC1–CC9
Yes
Any service using Claude
Availability
A1
No
Real-time or SLA-bound Claude endpoints
Processing Integrity
PI1
No
Claude outputs drive consequential decisions
Confidentiality
C1
No
Claude receives trade secrets or NDA-protected data
Privacy
P1–P8
No
Claude processes personal information (PII/PHI)

Practical guidance: Most enterprise Claude deployments must elect at least Security + Availability + Confidentiality. Add Processing Integrity if Claude outputs affect pricing, lending, or HR decisions. Add Privacy if the prompts can include user-identifiable data.
Common Criteria — Security (CC1–CC9)
The Common Criteria are organized into nine series. The table below maps each CC series to the corresponding Claude operational control:
CC Series
Criterion Name
Claude Control
Key Evidence
CC1
Control Environment
Claude API usage policy; executive sign-off on AI governance charter
Signed AI acceptable-use policy; org chart showing AI ownership
CC2
Communication & Information
Claude usage dashboards surfaced to stakeholders; system prompt change notifications
Screenshot of usage dashboard; change-log emails
CC3
Risk Assessment
Quarterly Claude risk registry review; new-use-case risk intake form
Completed risk intake forms; risk register with residual scores
CC4
Monitoring Activities
Automated token-usage anomaly alerts; weekly sampling of Claude outputs
Alert configuration exports; sampling review tickets
CC5
Control Activities
Mandatory operator system prompt review gate; red-team approval for new system prompts
PR review history; red-team sign-off records
CC6
Logical Access Controls
API key scoped to minimum-necessary permissions; key rotation every 90 days
Key rotation log; IAM policy JSON exports
CC7
System Operations
Claude model version pinning; production deployment gate requiring tested model version
Deployment pipeline configs; model version manifests
CC8
Change Management
System prompt change control process with rollback procedure
Change tickets; rollback runbook; approval records
CC9
Risk Mitigation
Claude output filtering layer; PII scrubbing before logging
Filter rule-set version history; DLP configuration exports
CC6 — Logical Access Controls in Practice
CC6 receives the most auditor scrutiny for Claude deployments because API keys are the primary access vector. Auditors want evidence of:
Key provisioning tied to a service account with a documented purpose
Automated key rotation — 90 days is the most common control standard
Revocation within one business day of personnel departure
No shared keys across production and non-production environments

import anthropic, datetime, boto3

# Retrieve API key from AWS Secrets Manager — never hardcode
def get_claude_key(secret_name: str) -> str:
    sm = boto3.client("secretsmanager")
    resp = sm.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_name)
    return resp["SecretString"]

# Rotation helper — call from CI pipeline every 90 days
def rotate_api_key(secret_name: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic):
    # Anthropic does not expose a key-rotation API; rotation must be done in
    # the Anthropic Console and the new key stored in Secrets Manager.
    # This function logs the event for audit evidence.
    entry = {
        "event": "api_key_rotation",
        "secret": secret_name,
        "rotated_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "rotated_by": "rotation-lambda",
    }
    print(json.dumps(entry))          # ships to CloudWatch → SIEM
    return entry
CC7 — System Operations: Model Version Pinning

Pinning the Claude model version ensures the system behaviour auditors tested does not silently change when Anthropic releases a new model. The model string is stored in configuration, not embedded in application code, so it can be rotated through the change management process (CC8) when a model upgrade is approved.

# config.py — version controlled; changes require change ticket
CLAUDE_CONFIG = {
    "model": "claude-opus-4-8",   # pinned; upgrade requires CC8 change ticket
    "max_tokens": 2048,
    "temperature": 0,             # deterministic outputs for audit reproducibility
}

# audit_log.py
import json, datetime

def log_request(prompt_hash: str, model: str, token_usage: dict):
    """Emit structured log line for CC4 monitoring and PI1 completeness."""
    record = {
        "ts": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "model": model,
        "prompt_sha256": prompt_hash,
        "input_tokens": token_usage.get("input_tokens"),
        "output_tokens": token_usage.get("output_tokens"),
    }
    print(json.dumps(record))
Availability (A1)
A1 requires that the system is available for operation and use as committed. For Claude-backed services with uptime SLAs, this translates to:
Documented availability commitment (e.g., 99.5% monthly uptime) in your service's terms
Circuit-breaker logic that surfaces a degraded-mode response if the Anthropic API returns 5xx errors, rather than propagating the failure silently
Uptime monitoring dashboards accessible to the SOC 2 auditor
Incident runbook covering Anthropic API unavailability scenarios

import anthropic, time

class ClaudeWithCircuitBreaker:
    def __init__(self, threshold: int = 3, timeout: int = 60):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic()
        self.failures = 0
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.open_until = 0

    def complete(self, system: str, user_msg: str) -> str:
        if time.time() < self.open_until:
            # Circuit open — return graceful degraded response
            return "[Service temporarily unavailable. Please retry in a moment.]"
        try:
            resp = self.client.messages.create(
                model="claude-opus-4-8",
                max_tokens=1024,
                system=system,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}],
            )
            self.failures = 0
            return resp.content[0].text
        except anthropic.APIStatusError as e:
            self.failures += 1
            if self.failures >= self.threshold:
                self.open_until = time.time() + self.timeout
                # Emit A1 availability event for audit log
                print(json.dumps({"event": "circuit_open", "failures": self.failures}))
            raise
Processing Integrity (PI1)

PI1 requires that system processing is complete, valid, accurate, timely, and authorized. For Claude, this criterion is most relevant when model outputs feed downstream business logic — a credit decision, a medical triage recommendation, or an automated content moderation action.
Controls to assert:
Every Claude call is logged with a cryptographic hash of the prompt so outputs can be attributed to a specific input
Output validation layer rejects responses that fail a schema check before they enter the business process
Sampling review: a defined percentage of Claude outputs is reviewed by a human each week
Completeness check: no request is silently discarded — every token_usage event is reconciled against the request log daily

import hashlib, json, anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def call_with_pi_controls(system: str, user: str) -> dict:
    prompt_hash = hashlib.sha256((system + user).encode()).hexdigest()
    
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
    )
    output_text = resp.content[0].text
    output_hash = hashlib.sha256(output_text.encode()).hexdigest()

    # PI1 audit record — every field is required evidence
    pi_record = {
        "request_id": resp.id,
        "prompt_sha256": prompt_hash,
        "output_sha256": output_hash,
        "model": resp.model,
        "stop_reason": resp.stop_reason,
        "input_tokens": resp.usage.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": resp.usage.output_tokens,
        "complete": resp.stop_reason == "end_turn",  # PI1 completeness flag
    }
    print(json.dumps(pi_record))     # route to audit log sink
    return {"text": output_text, "pi": pi_record}
Confidentiality (C1)

C1 requires that confidential information is protected as committed. When Claude receives confidential business data — unreleased earnings, M&A deal memos, proprietary research — the confidentiality controls must ensure that data does not leak via logs, model training, or inadvertent responses.
Risk
Control
Evidence Artifact
Confidential prompt content lands in application logs
PII/secrets scrubber runs before logging prompt text; prompt hash logged instead of plaintext
DLP tool configuration; scrubber unit-test results
Model training on confidential inputs
Anthropic API terms prohibit using API input for training by default; confirm BAA or enterprise data agreement is in place
Executed Anthropic data processing agreement
Confidential data in conversation history returned to other users
Conversation context is user-scoped; no cross-user context leakage in API calls
Code review records; integration test suite results
Downstream log aggregation captures confidential content
Log sinks configured to exclude 'claude_prompt_plaintext' field; retention limited to 30 days
Log pipeline configuration export; retention policy document
Privacy (P1–P8)

The Privacy criteria map to eight privacy practices derived from the AICPA's Generally Accepted Privacy Principles (GAPP). When Claude processes personal information, all eight are potentially in scope:
Criterion
Privacy Practice
Claude-Specific Control
P1
Notice
System prompt includes data-processing notice seen by end users before they submit personal data
P2
Choice & Consent
Consent flag checked in session metadata before any PII-bearing message is forwarded to Claude
P3
Collection
Input validation strips collection of unnecessary PII before prompt construction
P4
Use, Retention & Disposal
PII retention schedule enforced by data pipeline; Claude conversation logs purged per schedule
P5
Access
DSAR workflow exports all Claude interaction logs for a data subject within 30 days
P6
Disclosure to Third Parties
Anthropic DPA executed; sub-processor list published and updated quarterly
P7
Quality
PII accuracy check — Claude is not used to generate, update, or infer PII without human review
P8
Monitoring & Enforcement
Monthly privacy metrics reviewed in governance committee; breach response runbook tested annually

# Privacy-aware Claude wrapper implementing P2 (consent) and P3 (collection minimization)

import re, anthropic

PII_REGEX = re.compile(
    r'\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b'           # SSN
    r'|\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}\b'  # email
    r'|\b(?:\+1[\s-]?)?\(?\d{3}\)?[\s.-]?\d{3}[\s.-]?\d{4}\b'  # US phone
)

def privacy_safe_call(
    prompt: str,
    user_has_consented: bool,
    system: str = "You are a helpful assistant.",
) -> str:
    if not user_has_consented:
        raise ValueError("P2 violation: cannot send PII to Claude without user consent.")
    
    # P3: strip PII that is not necessary for the task
    clean_prompt = PII_REGEX.sub("[PII-REDACTED]", prompt)
    if clean_prompt != prompt:
        print(json.dumps({"event": "pii_stripped", "original_len": len(prompt)}))

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": clean_prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text
Assembling the SOC 2 Control Matrix

Auditors expect a formal control matrix (often called a System Description Annex) that lists every TSC criterion in scope, the corresponding control, the control owner, and the evidence population. Maintain this as a version-controlled document:

# scripts/generate_soc2_matrix.py
# Outputs a CSV suitable for pasting into the auditor's provided template

import csv, sys

CONTROLS = [
    # (criterion, control_title, owner, evidence_type, evidence_location)
    ("CC6.1", "API Key Rotation", "Platform Eng", "Log", "CloudWatch/api-key-rotation"),
    ("CC6.2", "RBAC Policy Review", "IAM Team",    "Document", "Confluence/iam-policy-v{quarter}"),
    ("CC7.1", "Model Version Pin", "ML Ops",       "Config", "GitHub/config.py#CLAUDE_CONFIG"),
    ("CC8.1", "Prompt Change Control", "Security", "Ticket", "Jira/project=CLAUDE-CC"),
    ("CC9.1", "Output Filter Rules", "Security",   "Config", "GitHub/filters/content_rules.json"),
    ("A1.1",  "Uptime SLA Monitor", "SRE",         "Dashboard", "Datadog/claude-availability"),
    ("PI1.1", "PI Audit Log",       "Platform Eng","Log", "S3/pi-logs/{year}/{month}"),
    ("C1.1",  "PII Scrubber",       "Privacy Eng", "Code", "GitHub/log_safe.py"),
    ("P2.1",  "Consent Check",      "Privacy Eng", "Code", "GitHub/privacy_safe_call.py"),
    ("P4.1",  "Log Retention Policy","Privacy Eng","Document", "Confluence/data-retention"),
    ("P5.1",  "DSAR Workflow",      "Privacy Eng", "Process","Confluence/dsar-runbook"),
]

writer = csv.writer(sys.stdout)
writer.writerow(["Criterion","Control","Owner","Evidence Type","Evidence Location"])
for row in CONTROLS:
    writer.writerow(row)
Run this script quarterly and diff the output against the prior quarter. Any control removed or owner changed without a change ticket is a finding gap.
Type I vs. Type II Reports and Claude Evidence Windows

SOC 2 Type I reports on controls as-of a point in time; Type II reports on the operating effectiveness of controls over a period (typically 6–12 months). For Claude-backed services undergoing a Type II audit, the evidence population must cover the entire audit window:
API key rotation logs must show every rotation event in the period, not just the current key
Output sampling records must be dated throughout the window — a burst of reviews in the last week before the audit will not satisfy auditors
Model version change records must document every version change, the approver, and the reason
Incident records must include all API unavailability events and the corresponding resolution steps

Automate evidence collection from day one of a new audit window rather than reconstructing it retrospectively. A structured log pipeline (e.g., CloudWatch → S3 → Athena) makes it straightforward to export the entire population for any time range the auditor requests.
Common SOC 2 Gaps in Claude Deployments
Gap
Root Cause
Remediation
API key shared between prod and dev
Convenience during early development
Provision separate Anthropic Console workspaces; enforce via org policy
No formal model-upgrade change process
Engineers upgrade model string ad-hoc
Add model version to change management scope; require JIRA ticket for any change
Output logs contain PII
Logging added before scrubber was built
Retroactively apply scrubber to existing logs; purge raw PII fields per retention policy
No sampling review cadence
Assumed Claude self-monitors
Stand up weekly sampling process; document in runbook; assign owner
Anthropic DPA not executed
Legal not involved at procurement
Initiate DPA execution; add to vendor onboarding checklist
No documented system prompt change control
System prompts treated as config, not code
Move system prompts to version control; require PR + approval to merge
Summary

SOC 2 compliance for Claude deployments is achievable by mapping the five Trust Services Criteria to concrete operational controls: API key management for CC6, model version pinning for CC7, circuit breakers for A1, prompt/response hashing for PI1, PII scrubbing for C1, and consent checks for P2. The key to audit success is automated evidence generation — every control must produce a structured log record or configuration artifact that auditors can inspect and re-perform. Maintain a living control matrix, run it quarterly, and close gaps before the audit window opens rather than scrambling at the end.
